concepts_generated.product_role: The small forest green card pinned over the evening slots — 'AI Assistant Active' — is the product's entire presence. It replaces a standing absence with a standing response. No fanfare. Just a correction.
concepts_generated.visual_world: Warm oat-colored wall, large paper wall calendar with handwritten entries, pushpins, natural morning light from the side. The palette is oat, cream paper, navy handwriting, small forest green accent from the correction card. Lived-in but organized.
concepts_generated.ending_payoff: The calendar with the correction cards pinned over every evening slot. The text: 'Create your AI assistant.' The absence is replaced by a default the business actually wants.
concepts_generated.emotional_tone: Matter-of-fact, slightly dry — the emotion comes from the recognition that the absence was normalized, not from any dramatized wake-up moment.
concepts_generated.funnel_fit_note: Solution-aware audiences who have deferred action because no visible crisis has occurred will recognize their own normalized default in the calendar. The mechanism removes the requirement for a dramatic trigger.
production_risks: The handwriting in the evening slots must be legible on camera — prop preparation requires careful planning
The wall calendar must be large enough to read at camera distance without being obviously oversized
concepts_generated.why_stops_scroll: A paper wall calendar with repeating entries in the evening slots is visually familiar — but the content of those entries creates immediate cognitive friction. The viewer reads 'No website response available' where they expected a meeting name and stops.
concepts_generated.story_progression: The paper wall calendar is shown in full. The camera reads through the week — morning appointments, afternoon meetings, everything organized and deliberate. Then the camera focuses on the evening slots. Each one reads the same: 'No website response available.' The handwriting is neat. The entries look like any other appointment. A hand enters and traces the evening slots with a finger — Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, all the way through Sunday. The text: 'This isn't an emergency. It's your default.' The camera holds on the calendar. A second hand pins a small card over the evening slots — it reads 'AI Assistant Active.' The standing absence is replaced by a standing presence. No drama. No crisis. Just a correction to a default that was never examined.
concepts_generated.emotional_mechanism: The normalization inversion works through the calendar format — the standing appointment is read as deliberate and organized before its content is understood as a structural failure. The business didn't make a mistake; it simply never examined a default.
concepts_generated.opening_two_seconds: A large paper wall calendar in sharp focus — appointment slots visible, a hand flipping to the current week. The camera reads the evening slots before any text appears.
characters_or_hero_objects: The large paper wall calendar
The repeating evening slot entries
The finger tracing the pattern
The correction card pinned over the absences

concepts_generated.title: The Door That's Always Locked After Six
concepts_generated.pacing: Slow. The first image holds long enough for the viewer to feel the locked door. The hand at the glass is brief. The second image is the resolution — also held without rush.
concepts_generated.hook_line: The door's locked. The lights are still on. And someone's standing outside with a question.
concepts_generated.viewpoint: Exterior, looking in — the viewer's perspective is the visitor's perspective. This is unusual for business content and creates immediate identification.

atmosphere.time_of_day: Early evening — past business hours, sky still holding blue-grey light
atmosphere.palette_intent: Warm amber interior against cool blue-grey exterior — the contrast is the entire emotional argument
atmosphere.lighting_intent: Interior warm ambient light visible through glass, exterior cool dusk — the warmth of the interior makes the inaccessibility of the locked door more acute

fingerprint.hero_object: The locked door with the warm interior visible behind it — inaccessibility against warmth
fingerprint.core_premise: The locked door after hours is not an emergency — it is the ordinary, unremarkable baseline that every local business accepts without questioning whether the website is the same door.
fingerprint.visual_world: Brick or stone local business exterior, warm amber interior light, glass door with 'Closed' sign, early evening sky
fingerprint.emotional_arc: Familiar recognition → The hand at the glass → The normalization of the locked door → Quiet resolution through the placard
fingerprint.ending_mechanism: The same door, same evening, same locked exterior — with the placard as the only change. The door is still closed. The website isn't.
fingerprint.opening_mechanism: Exterior shot of a warm interior behind a locked 'Closed' door — the viewer's perspective is the visitor's perspective before any text appears.
fingerprint.product_mechanism: The small placard beside the 'Closed' sign — the mechanism that allows a conversation even when the door is locked
fingerprint.creative_direction: Takes the worst-case consequence of ignoring the availability gap and strips away its dramatic framing to reveal that it isn't a catastrophe — it's the ordinary, unremarkable baseline that has already been running. The mechanism works through normalization inversion: the audience expects the worst-case to be a shocking event, but the mechanism reveals the worst case is simply the current state, repeating quietly every night.
fingerprint.palette_atmosphere: Warm amber interior, cool blue-grey exterior — the contrast is the emotional engine

concepts_generated.central_idea: A physical front door with a 'We're Open' sign flipped to 'Closed' — photographed from outside, looking in. The door is locked. The lights inside are on. The display is visible. Someone could want something. But the mechanism for getting it does not exist after six. The visual holds without commentary. Then a second image: the same door, same lights, same display — but a small placard reads 'Questions? Ask our assistant.' The door is still locked. The website is not.

creative_dna.world: The exterior of a local service business in the early evening — the familiar, unremarkable moment of after-hours inaccessibility that every business has accepted as normal
creative_dna.productRole: The small placard — not a replacement for the door, but the mechanism that allows the conversation the door cannot have
creative_dna.coreConflict: The business has accepted the locked door as inevitable and has never questioned whether the website is equally locked
creative_dna.endingIntent: The viewer should feel the placard as a small, obvious correction to a normalized default — not a transformation, just a fix
creative_dna.mainCharacter: The locked door — the ordinary, accepted default that is also the structural problem
immutableRules: The door must remain locked in the final image — the product doesn't open the door, it opens the conversation
The interior light must be warm — the inaccessibility is more acute against warmth
No dramatic visitor character — just the hand at the glass, briefly
creative_dna.viewerQuestion: Is my website as locked as my front door right now?

concepts_generated.product_role: The small placard beside the 'Closed' sign — the product is not the open door, it is the mechanism that allows a conversation to happen even when the door is locked.
concepts_generated.visual_world: Exterior of a local service business — stone or brick facade, warm amber interior light visible through glass, physical 'Closed' sign. Evening light — not dark, just past business hours, the sky still holding some light. The palette is warm amber interior against cool blue-grey exterior dusk.
concepts_generated.ending_payoff: The same door, same evening, same locked exterior — but the placard signals that the conversation doesn't have to wait. The text: 'Create your AI assistant.' The door is still closed. The website isn't.
concepts_generated.emotional_tone: Quiet and observational — the emotion is in the warmth of the interior against the inaccessibility of the exterior. Not dramatic. Not a crisis. Just the ordinary reality of after-hours.
concepts_generated.funnel_fit_note: Local service businesses, beauty salons, car dealers, and service centers will recognize the closed door immediately as their own. The normalization mechanism works because they have accepted this reality without questioning the website equivalent.
production_risks: The exterior shot must be genuine — not a set — to carry the emotional weight of the familiar closed-door experience
The evening light must be calibrated carefully — too dark reads as ominous, too bright reads as daytime
concepts_generated.why_stops_scroll: The exterior shot of a warm, inviting interior behind a locked 'Closed' sign is viscerally familiar and immediately legible as a moment of missed connection. The hand at the glass makes the visitor's perspective physical before any text appears.
concepts_generated.story_progression: The exterior shot holds. The interior is warm, well-stocked, clearly a place someone would want to enter. The 'Closed' sign is lit. A hand appears at the glass — not dramatic, just present — the physical gesture of a visitor who arrived after hours. The hand drops. The visitor doesn't appear — just the hand, then its absence. The image holds on the locked door and the warm interior. Then the camera cuts to the same door — same time, same lighting — but a small additional placard has been added beside the 'Closed' sign. It reads: 'Your question won't wait until morning. Ours won't either.' The warm interior is now paired with a mechanism that can respond. The door is still locked. The conversation doesn't have to be.
concepts_generated.emotional_mechanism: The normalization inversion works through the familiar closed-door image — every business owner has seen this, accepted it, and never questioned whether the website is the same door. The second image reveals that the door being locked is not the problem — the website being equally locked is.
concepts_generated.opening_two_seconds: A physical front door with an illuminated 'Closed' sign — the interior lights still on, visible products or displays behind the glass — shot from outside, looking in. The warmth of the interior against the locked exterior is the entire opening.
characters_or_hero_objects: The locked glass door with 'Closed' sign
The warm interior visible behind the glass
The hand at the glass
The small additional placard

directions_selected.label: The Asymmetric Readiness Clock
directions_selected.why_fits: Directly addresses the 24/7 support pain point and the speed/availability reframe. Solution-aware audience can immediately map their own hours to the mechanism. The clock logic makes the structural problem feel engineering-precise rather than emotional.
directions_selected.mechanism: Exposes a mismatch between two clocks running simultaneously — the business's operating hours and the visitor's decision-making hours — and holds them against each other as incompatible systems. The mechanism does not dramatize a story; it simply makes the structural incompatibility visible as a diagram of coverage gaps. The worst-case consequence emerges from the gap itself: wherever the two clocks do not overlap is where the business is constitutionally unable to compete, regardless of quality.
directions_selected.diversity_note: Unlike the staffing gap (role-based logic) or the preparation-gap mechanism (readiness vs. arrival), this works through temporal coverage mapping — two systems whose operating windows don't align, creating a structural blind spot.
directions_selected.anti_repetition_note: No threshold metaphor, no pipe/investment metaphor, no parallel track comparison. The mechanism is about temporal non-overlap as a competitive structure, not about effort or preparation.

directions_selected.label: The Unwitnessed Defection
directions_selected.why_fits: Speaks directly to consulting firms, professional services, and agencies who are data-oriented and process-minded. The pain of losing leads to silence is amplified by the impossibility of diagnosing the loss. Fits solution-aware stage by making the hidden cost structurally legible.
directions_selected.mechanism: Focuses on the structural invisibility of the loss — not that the lead left, but that the business will never know it happened, cannot measure it, cannot learn from it, and cannot correct it. The mechanism works through epistemological logic: the worst-case consequence of ignoring the availability gap is not a visible failure the business can respond to, but an invisible one that produces no signal, no data, and no opportunity for correction. The business cannot improve what it cannot detect.
directions_selected.diversity_note: Unlike the competitor-transfer mechanism (which tracks where the lead went) or the proximity mechanism (which tracks the visitor's time on page), this focuses entirely on the business's side — the permanent information blackout that prevents any response or learning.
directions_selected.anti_repetition_note: Does not use spatial, role, staffing, threshold, or investment logic. The mechanism is epistemological — about the impossibility of knowing, not the drama of what happened.

directions_selected.label: Compounding Silence Tax
directions_selected.why_fits: Solution-aware audience understands the problem exists but hasn't calculated its true scale. The 24/7 staffing pain point is reframed as a cost that compounds daily rather than a one-time inconvenience. Connects the speed/availability angle to the irreversibility of accumulated loss.
directions_selected.mechanism: Frames inaction not as a single missed event but as a recurring cost that accumulates silently over time — each day the gap exists, the loss is not one incident but the same incident repeating. The mechanism works through accumulation logic: the worst-case consequence is not a dramatic failure but an invisible subtraction that has been running continuously, growing larger the longer it goes unexamined.
directions_selected.diversity_note: Unlike proximity-without-contact (single event, single visitor) or the staffing gap mechanism (role framing), this works through temporal accumulation — the horror is the duration, not the single instance.
directions_selected.anti_repetition_note: Previous directions captured single-event loss or structural absence. This mechanism is about volume over time — the same loss happening 30, 60, 90 times before the business owner ever notices.

directions_selected.label: Worst Outcome Normalization
directions_selected.why_fits: Solution-aware audience may be deferring action because they haven't experienced a visible disaster. This mechanism removes the requirement for a dramatic wake-up event and makes the current state itself the consequence. Fits the 24/7 pain point and the lead-loss angle without inventing a fictional crisis.
directions_selected.mechanism: Takes the worst-case consequence of ignoring the availability gap and strips away its dramatic framing to reveal that it isn't a catastrophe — it's the ordinary, unremarkable baseline that has already been running. The mechanism works through normalization inversion: the audience expects the worst-case to be a shocking event, but the mechanism reveals the worst case is simply the current state, repeating quietly every night. The horror is that it requires no dramatic trigger — it is already the default.
directions_selected.diversity_note: Unlike accumulation logic (d1) which emphasizes growth over time, this mechanism emphasizes that the worst case requires no escalation — it is already present and requires no further development to qualify as the consequence.
directions_selected.anti_repetition_note: Avoids all spatial, role, proximity, and investment metaphors from recent directions. The mechanism is logical-definitional: redefining what 'worst case' means rather than illustrating it through a scenario.

evaluations.critic_notes: Accumulation logic is a reliable mechanism for solution-aware audiences who have normalized the problem. The 'silent subtraction' framing is strong because it reframes inaction as an ongoing event rather than a past failure. Slightly adjacent to d4 (both deal with the current state as the consequence) but the temporal compounding distinguishes it meaningfully. Risk: can feel abstract if not anchored to a concrete unit of loss. High production feasibility — the mechanism translates easily to copy, visuals, or a simple counter metaphor.

The philosophical-categorical framing is genuinely distinct from all recent directions and from peers in this set. Naming the irreversibility of intent-moments as a category is intellectually sharp and fits consultants and agencies well. However, the abstraction level is high — 'the nature of the loss' is harder to make visceral in short-form ad formats than spatial or temporal mechanisms. Emotional range is narrower because it operates in the register of logic rather than feeling. Feasibility is lower because the mechanism requires more cognitive setup than most short-form formats allow. Strong for long-form or written content; riskier for video or display.

The dual-clock / temporal coverage map is the most structurally precise mechanism in the set and maps directly onto the core angle (speed and availability as a competitive game). It avoids every metaphor used in recent directions and in peers. The 'engineering-precise' register is a strong differentiator for the SMB and professional services audience who respond to systems thinking. Highly producible — a literal clock or coverage grid visual is immediately legible. The mechanism is also the most directly actionable: the gap is visible, the fix is obvious, the urgency is structural rather than emotional. Top-tier direction.

Normalization inversion is psychologically sophisticated — removing the requirement for a dramatic wake-up event is a strong move for solution-aware audiences who have been deferring action. The 'worst case is already running' reframe is emotionally resonant in a quiet, unsettling way. Slightly overlaps with d1 in that both locate the consequence in the present/ongoing state, but d4 is definitional rather than accumulative — a meaningful distinction. The mechanism is versatile across formats. Slightly lower diversity vs. peers because the 'current state as consequence' logic has some adjacency to the staffing gap and preparation-gap directions already used.

The epistemological mechanism — you cannot improve what you cannot detect — is the most intellectually original direction in the set and has no analog in recent directions. It speaks precisely to data-oriented professional services and agency audiences. The 'permanent information blackout' framing is genuinely unsettling in a way that doesn't require drama. Production feasibility is moderate: the mechanism is abstract and requires careful execution to avoid feeling academic. Emotional range is narrower — operates in dread-of-ignorance rather than urgency or loss. Strong pairing candidate with a more visceral direction.

Decoupling qualification from capture is a clean, operationally precise mechanism that will land well with marketing agencies and consultants who think in systems. The insight that visitor quality is irrelevant without a capture system is genuinely useful and reframes the problem without repeating any recent direction's logic. Slightly lower originality because 'you built the funnel but not the net' is a familiar structural critique in marketing circles. Diversity vs. peers is moderate — it shares some conceptual territory with the preparation-gap direction (two systems, one built) though the decoupling logic is distinct. Solid but not the strongest in the set.

Passive-action equivalence is a strong rhetorical mechanism and fits the competitive framing of the angle well. 'Inaction is a gift to competitors' is emotionally activating for business owners. However, it shares logical territory with the competitor-transfer direction already used (both locate the consequence in competitor gain) — the distinction (business-side framing vs. lead-journey framing) is real but may not be perceptible enough in execution. Anti-repetition score reflects this adjacency risk. High production feasibility. Strong but not the most differentiated option given the existing direction pool.

direction_evaluation.selection_reason: d3 is the strongest single direction — its temporal coverage-gap mechanism is structurally precise, maps directly onto the speed/availability angle, avoids all recent direction logic, and is highly producible across formats. d5 provides maximum diversity through its epistemological mechanism (the business cannot detect the loss, therefore cannot correct it), which operates in a completely different register from all other directions and all recent work — it is the only direction in the set that focuses on the information blackout rather than the loss event itself. d1 adds the accumulation/compounding dimension — the same loss repeating silently over time — which is temporally distinct from d3's coverage-gap logic and emotionally distinct from d5's dread-of-ignorance. d4 rounds out the shortlist with normalization inversion — redefining the current state as the worst case already in progress — which operates in a definitional register distinct from d3 (structural), d5 (epistemological), and d1 (temporal). Together the four directions cover: structural incompatibility, information invisibility, temporal accumulation, and definitional reframing — four genuinely different communication mechanisms with no meaningful overlap.

directions_generated.label: Qualification Without Capture
directions_generated.why_fits: Directly addresses the lead-capture strength of the product and the pain of losing leads despite having the right audience. Fits marketing agencies, consultants, and professional services who spend on targeting and SEO. Reframes lead capture as a distinct system requirement, not a byproduct of traffic quality.
directions_generated.mechanism: Separates two things the business treats as linked — a visitor being qualified and a visitor being captured — and reveals that qualification without capture produces nothing. The mechanism works through decoupling logic: the business invests heavily in attracting the right visitors, but the worst-case consequence is that a perfectly qualified visitor can arrive, evaluate, and leave without producing any output the business can act on. Qualification is a property of the visitor; capture requires a system. Only one was built.
directions_generated.diversity_note: Unlike the preparation-gap mechanism (readiness for arrival vs. readiness for interaction), this decouples visitor quality from capture outcome — the visitor's qualification is irrelevant if no capture mechanism exists. Different structural logic.
directions_generated.anti_repetition_note: No investment/pipe metaphor, no staffing/role metaphor, no threshold/empty-room metaphor. The mechanism is operational-systems logic: two outputs that were assumed to be coupled are revealed to be independent.

directions_generated.label: The Default Competitor Advantage
directions_generated.why_fits: Fits the speed/availability reframe perfectly — the business that responds first wins not because it is better but because the others chose not to compete at that moment. Speaks to solution-aware audience who understand the competitive landscape but haven't framed availability as a competitive decision.
directions_generated.mechanism: Reveals that by doing nothing, the business has already made an active choice — it has chosen to give competitor websites a structural advantage by default. The mechanism works through passive-action equivalence: inaction in an availability competition is not neutral, it is a unilateral concession. The worst-case consequence is that the business's silence is not passive — it is functionally an endorsement of whichever competitor does respond. Not acting is a decision with a winner.
directions_generated.diversity_note: Unlike the competitor-transfer mechanism (which follows the lead's journey) or the worst-outcome normalization (which redefines the current state), this mechanism frames inaction as an active competitive choice with a beneficiary — the competitor who does act.
directions_generated.anti_repetition_note: The recent competitor-transfer direction followed the lead from one business to another. This mechanism stays entirely on the business's side and frames their inaction as a structural gift to competitors — different logical position, different communication mechanism.

ctaMatch.packageCta: Create your AI assistant — start capturing what the blank field never could.

candidateScores.coreIdea: A consulting firm's analytics dashboard — rendered as a physical printed report on a clean desk — shows every metric the business tracks: sessions, bounce rate, time on page. One row is blank. The label reads: 'Visitors who left with their question unanswered.' The data field is empty. Not zero. Empty. The point is that the loss cannot be measured because the system has no mechanism to detect it.
candidateScores.hookLine: You track everything. Except the thing that's costing you the most.
candidateScores.openingSituation: Extreme close-up on a printed analytics report being slid across a pale eucalyptus surface — fingers on the paper, the header visible, the camera tilting down to reveal a single blank row at the bottom.

candidateScores.coreIdea: A physical visitor log — the kind used at reception desks — sits open on a polished stone surface. Every page of the log is filled with names, times, reasons for visit. Then the camera reveals a second log labeled 'Website Visitors.' Every page is blank. The log exists. The entries do not. The business has been receiving visitors at a second entrance for years and has never once recorded who came through.
candidateScores.hookLine: You know everyone who walked through your front door. You have no idea who visited your website.
candidateScores.openingSituation: A leather-bound visitor log snapped open flat on a polished stone surface — pages fanning to reveal dense handwritten entries, then the camera cuts to an identical log beside it, opening to blank white pages.

comparativeJudge.winnerReason: c3 wins on originality, funnel fit, and natural product integration. The blank data field within a real analytics report is a more precise and more disturbing execution of the structural-invisibility argument than the two-log parallel in c4. The epistemic move — not a bad number, but the absence of a number — is genuinely uncommon in ad creative and will stop a data-literate audience more effectively than a familiar filled/empty contrast device. The product integration is tighter: the AI assistant fills in the blank field structurally, which maps directly to the product's function of capturing conversations that previously produced no record. The eucalyptus-cream-graphite palette is fresh against the full fingerprint history. The stop-scroll is marginally softer than c4 but the payoff depth is higher, and the scoring rubric correctly weights originality, funnel fit, and product integration against stop-scroll alone. c4 is a strong concept but operates at a slightly higher level of metaphorical abstraction and uses a more conventional contrast structure.

selectedCandidate.ending: The blank field is the entire argument. The product doesn't dramatize — it fills in what was always structurally absent.

The point is that the loss cannot be measured because the system has no mechanism to detect it.

creativeDNA.productRole: The mechanism that fills the blank — not by fixing a number, but by creating the data that was always structurally absent
creativeDNA.coreConflict: The business has built a measurement system for everything it can control — and the thing it cannot measure is the thing that is costing it the most
creativeDNA.endingIntent: The viewer should feel the product as a diagnostic correction — it gives them information they have never had access to
creativeDNA.mainCharacter: The blank field — the metric that has never existed and cannot exist without structural change
immutableRules: The blank field must be visually empty, not numerically zero
No dramatized failure — the blank is the entire argument
The product must be introduced as a data mechanism, not as a chatbot widget
creativeDNA.viewerQuestion: What is in my blank field? How large is the number I've never been able to see?

selectedCandidate.visualPromise: Pale eucalyptus wood surface, printed paper analytics report, mechanical pencil, single overhead pendant light with a warm white bulb. The palette is eucalyptus green, cream paper, graphite pencil mark. No screens. No blue light. Atmosphere: Indeterminate — pendant light suggests focused work, not a specific hour, Eucalyptus green, cream paper, graphite — cool, quiet, analytical, Single overhead pendant, warm white — enough to read the report clearly, intimate enough to feel like a private discovery. Viewpoint: Near-overhead angle tilting down — the viewer reads the report from the same angle as the person holding it.. Pacing: Slow pan down the report. The blank field is held for two full seconds before the pencil circle appears. No rushing the moment of recognition..
selectedCandidate.openingSituation: Extreme close-up on a printed analytics report being slid across a pale eucalyptus surface — fingers on the paper, the header visible, the camera tilting down to reveal a single blank row at the bottom.
selectedCandidate.storyProgression: The printed analytics report is slid into frame. The camera reads down the rows: sessions tracked, bounce rate tracked, conversion tracked. Then the camera reaches the final row. The label is there. The data field is blank — not a zero, not a dash, but genuinely empty white space. A hand enters frame and circles the blank field with a pencil. A text overlay: 'You can't improve what you can't detect.' The report is then flipped over — the back is blank. Another text overlay: 'Every night, visitors leave with a question you'll never know they had.' The final frame introduces the product — not as a dashboard, but as the mechanism that begins to fill in the blank: an AI assistant that captures the conversation the website was previously unable to have.
selectedCandidate.emotionalReaction: Quiet and precise — the discomfort comes from the blankness, not from any dramatized failure. via The blank field is epistemologically disturbing for data-oriented audiences. It is not a bad number — it is the absence of a number. The business cannot know how large the loss is, which makes the loss irreducible by analysis alone.
selectedCandidate.productConnection: The AI assistant is introduced as the mechanism that produces the data the blank field was always missing — not by tracking failure, but by converting those conversations into captured leads the business can finally see.

conceptFingerprint.core_premise: The loss cannot be measured because no system exists to detect it — the blank field in the analytics report is the structural proof that the business is flying blind on its most significant recurring loss.

Overhead natural light from a skylight — bright and clear, not moody. Atmosphere: Midday skylight — bright, neutral, archival, Dark slate, cream pages, burgundy leather, black ink — serious and archival, no warmth, Overhead skylight — flat, clear, no shadows that obscure the pages. Viewpoint: Near-overhead, slightly angled — the viewer sees both logs simultaneously in the frame.. Pacing: Deliberate. The first log is shown long enough to register its density. The second log is held long enough for the blankness to become uncomfortable. The pen stopping is the pivot point..
generatedCandidates.openingSituation: A leather-bound visitor log snapped open flat on a polished stone surface — pages fanning to reveal dense handwritten entries, then the camera cuts to an identical log beside it, opening to blank white pages.
generatedCandidates.storyProgression: The physical reception visitor log opens to show pages of recorded visits — names, times, purposes, outcomes. The camera lingers long enough to register the density of information. Then it cuts to an identical log placed beside it. This one opens to blank pages. No entries. The label on the spine reads 'Website Visitors.' The camera holds on the blank pages. A hand enters and writes the date at the top of the first blank page — and then stops. No name to write. No question to record. No outcome to note. The pen is set down. A text overlay: 'They came. They had a question. They left. You'll never know.' The final frame introduces the product as the mechanism that begins populating the second log — not with phantom data, but with real captured conversations and leads the website was previously unable to record.
generatedCandidates.emotionalReaction: Composed, almost archival — the emotion is in the structural contrast, not in any dramatized moment. via The contrast between the dense, filled log and the blank log is immediately legible as a structural failure. The business invested in recording one type of visitor and built no equivalent system for the other. The pen stopping mid-entry makes the absence physical.
generatedCandidates.productConnection: The product is introduced as the mechanism that begins filling the second log — the AI assistant captures visitor questions and converts them to leads, making the previously invisible record of website visits finally legible.

selectionDiagnostics.whyWon: critic: c3 wins on originality, funnel fit, and natural product integration. The blank data field within a real analytics report is a more precise and more disturbing execution of the structural-invisibility argument than the two-log parallel in c4. The epistemic move — not a bad number, but the absence of a number — is genuinely uncommon in ad creative and will stop a data-literate audience more effectively than a familiar filled/empty contrast device. The product integration is tighter: the AI assistant fills in the blank field structurally, which maps directly to the product's function of capturing conversations that previously produced no record. The eucalyptus-cream-graphite palette is fresh against the full fingerprint history. The stop-scroll is marginally softer than c4 but the payoff depth is higher, and the scoring rubric correctly weights originality, funnel fit, and product integration against stop-scroll alone. c4 is a strong concept but operates at a slightly higher level of metaphorical abstraction and uses a more conventional contrast structure.

opening.first_spoken_guidance: Open with an immediate reaction using Dilemma — not context or setup. The opening spoken thought must be one complete meaning unit (one short phrase, or two ultra-short phrases) — not an unfinished setup. The stored hook MUST be the same thought as the first spoken line — do not dilute a strong hook into a weaker voiceover setup. Open mid-choice. Name both options in concrete terms. Make the cost of either side feel real. Narrative seed: Unexpected but relevant: Open mid-choice. Name both options in concrete terms. Make the cost of either side feel real. Keep the link to Reframe lead capture as a speed and availability game. The prospect who visits three competing websites at 10 PM will contact the one that answers their question. Not the one with the best branding or the longest track record. Availability wins. Connect to the pain of losing leads to competitors simply because the website was silent. / Unable to answer customer questions when offline clear by the next beat.
opening.first_visual_guidance: The first visual is an attention event with clear meaning — not a decorative sentence illustration. Show a frozen decision moment with consequences visible — not a notebook vs paper gag. Preferred opening visual concept: Hand hovering over a packed suitcase while a glowing "post now" reminder buzzes about Fenrik.chat Reject low-information openings: frames that add no stakes, curiosity, contrast, or situation meaning. Calm or empty frames are fine when absence/stakes ARE the meaning; interchangeable stock staging with no situation is not. Render coherently via Visual Narrative / Medium / Profile / Identity — attention chooses the idea; Identity is treatment only (never relocate the event).
opening.first_subtitle_guidance: First subtitle mirrors the first spoken thought — same words, no softer paraphrase.

phases.delivery: Opening: empathetic frustration at the problem, not the viewer.
Body: conversational contrast — short sentences, not one long equally-paced paragraph.
Emphasis: slight lift on the key turn, contradiction, or insight line.
Brief pause before the reveal, punchline, or payoff.
Payoff: Slightly tense, intimate, as if the viewer is inside the choice.
Close: satisfying landing; CTA only if present, never aggressive.

delivery_arc.tts_instruction_fragment: Opening: empathetic frustration at the problem, not the viewer. Then settle into conversational body delivery. Use spoken rhythm: short sentences, contrast, and a brief pause before the reveal. Emphasize the turn or punchline — do not read every clause at the same energy. Avoid long equally-paced paragraphs; land one idea per breath. Slightly tense, intimate, as if the viewer is inside the choice.

metaphor_clarity.guidance:
METAPHOR CLARITY STANDARD (deterministic — keep the metaphor, explain sooner):
- A first-time viewer may not decode what the opening metaphor represents
  within the first third of the video.
- Do NOT drop the creative metaphor.
- By the end of SETUP (first ~1/3), the spoken voiceover MUST name the concrete
  product problem the metaphor stands for (e.g. unanswered website visitors / missed leads).
- Scene 1 may stay metaphorical; scene 2 + narration must make the referent obvious.
- Prefer: metaphor image → spoken bridge ('this is your website…') → consequence → solution.
- Product bridge to surface in the first third: The AI assistant is introduced as the mechanism that produces the data the blank field was always missing — not by tracking failure, but by converting those conversations into captured leads the business can finally see.
- Core idea to land early: A consulting firm's analytics dashboard — rendered as a physical printed report on a clean desk — shows every metric the business tracks: sessions, bounce rate, time on page. One row is blank. The label reads: 'Visitors who left with their question unanswered.' The data field is empty. Not zero. Empty. The point is that the loss cannot be measured because the system has no mechanism to detect it.
- Director framing: Situation first: film a person walking away unanswered / waiting for a reply — NOT a paper boat, closed notebook, or abstract prop standing in for the visitor.

creative_candidate.openingSituation: Extreme close-up on a printed analytics report being slid across a pale eucalyptus surface — fingers on the paper, the header visible, the camera tilting down to reveal a single blank row at the bottom.
creative_candidate.storyProgression: The printed analytics report is slid into frame. The camera reads down the rows: sessions tracked, bounce rate tracked, conversion tracked. Then the camera reaches the final row. The label is there. The data field is blank — not a zero, not a dash, but genuinely empty white space. A hand enters frame and circles the blank field with a pencil. A text overlay: 'You can't improve what you can't detect.' The report is then flipped over — the back is blank. Another text overlay: 'Every night, visitors leave with a question you'll never know they had.' The final frame introduces the product — not as a dashboard, but as the mechanism that begins to fill in the blank: an AI assistant that captures the conversation the website was previously unable to have.
creative_candidate.productConnection: The AI assistant is introduced as the mechanism that produces the data the blank field was always missing — not by tracking failure, but by converting those conversations into captured leads the business can finally see.

timeline_debug.corrective_guidance:
METAPHOR CLARITY STANDARD (deterministic — keep the metaphor, explain sooner):
- A first-time viewer may not decode what the opening metaphor represents
  within the first third of the video.
- Do NOT drop the creative metaphor.
- By the end of SETUP (first ~1/3), the spoken voiceover MUST name the concrete
  product problem the metaphor stands for (e.g. unanswered website visitors / missed leads).
- Scene 1 may stay metaphorical; scene 2 + narration must make the referent obvious.
- Prefer: metaphor image → spoken bridge ('this is your website…') → consequence → solution.
- Product bridge to surface in the first third: The AI assistant is introduced as the mechanism that produces the data the blank field was always missing — not by tracking failure, but by converting those conversations into captured leads the business can finally see.
- Core idea to land early: A consulting firm's analytics dashboard — rendered as a physical printed report on a clean desk — shows every metric the business tracks: sessions, bounce rate, time on page. One row is blank. The label reads: 'Visitors who left with their question unanswered.' The data field is empty. Not zero. Empty. The point is that the loss cannot be measured because the system has no mechanism to detect it.
- Director framing: Situation first: film a person walking away unanswered / waiting for a reply — NOT a paper boat, closed notebook, or abstract prop standing in for the visitor.

creative.tts_instructions: Speak naturally for a short vertical social video. Language: en. Tone: Simple and accessible; Direct and action-oriented; Transparent and honest; Friendly and approachable; Concise and practical. Read the script exactly; do not add or skip words. Delivery: clear, confident, practical. Delivery: warm, intimate, conversational storytelling pace. Delivery: confident, concise, not aggressive. Opening: empathetic frustration at the problem, not the viewer. Then settle into conversational body deliver

image_prompts: Extreme close-up photorealistic portrait 9:16 vertical frame. A printed analytics report — rows of data visible as structured columns, no readable text — is being slid across a pale eucalyptus wood surface. Two fingers hold the lower edge of the paper. The camera angle is near-overhead, tilting slightly down toward the bottom of the report where the final row sits visibly empty: the column cells are blank white, while every row above them contains printed marks. Overcast diffused daylight from above, muted earth tones, cream paper, graphite marks, eucalyptus green surface. A single warm overhead pendant light casts a soft focused pool. Foreground framing: the edge of a ceramic cup partially visible at the left border. Implied human presence — hands only, no face. Curious, alert atmosphere. No readable words, numbers, or labels anywhere in the frame.

Close detail photorealistic portrait 9:16 vertical frame. A hand holds a mechanical pencil, drawing a deliberate circle around the blank bottom row of a printed analytics report resting on a pale eucalyptus wood surface. The circled area is visibly empty — white space surrounded by a pencil ring — while the rows above it show printed data marks. The pencil tip is sharp and precise. Overcast diffused daylight, muted earth tones, cream paper, graphite. Near-overhead angle tilted slightly toward the hand. Foreground framing: a faint shadow of a shelf edge at the top of the frame. No readable text, numbers, or labels in the frame. Quiet, alert, analytical mood.

Photorealistic portrait 9:16 vertical frame. The analytics report has been flipped over on the pale eucalyptus surface. The back of the paper faces up — completely blank, no marks, no print, no data. The pencil rests beside it, unused. The surface around the paper is clean and bare. Overcast diffused daylight from above, muted earth tones, cream paper. Near-overhead angle. Foreground framing: the edge of the wooden surface visible at the lower frame boundary. Implied human presence — fingertips just visible at the top edge of the paper as if they just released it. The blankness is the entire subject. No readable text or labels anywhere.

Photorealistic portrait 9:16 vertical frame. A laptop sits open on the same pale eucalyptus wood surface, screen facing three-quarter toward the viewer. On the laptop screen: a clean chat interface showing a live conversation between a website visitor and an AI assistant — structured message bubbles visible as a recognizable chat layout, no readable text. The printed analytics report from earlier is visible in the soft background, slightly out of focus, the pencil circle still faintly visible. Overcast diffused daylight, muted earth tones, warm amber highlight on the laptop lid from the overhead pendant. Foreground framing: a plant stem at the left frame edge. Three-quarter angle, subject centered with natural headroom. Curious, alert resolution mood. No readable text on screen.

You can paste your URL and watch an AI assistant answer questions using your existing content — no account, no setup, no decision required yet. The only thing stopping you from seeing it is not trying it.

brief.angle: Close with urgency grounded in the everyday reality of lost leads. Tonight, while the office is closed, someone will visit your website with a real question. What happens next is already decided by whether your website can respond. Anchor to the pain of after-hours lead loss. End with a direct, clear path: create your AI assistant and let your website answer while you're unavailable.
